ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for battered deep-fried shrimp; closest_parent 'shrimp food product' (FOODON_03302051) used as the nearest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJW covers shrimps and prawns as a category; no specific code for battered deep-fried shrimp exists in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of crispy battered shrimp consists of approximately 4 to 5 large shrimp with coating, weighing around 150 g, as served in a restaurant portion.

nutrient_source.title: Fried shrimp, breaded — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB No. 15150)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for breaded fried shrimp used as the primary nutrient reference. Values represent shrimp with a wheat-flour or breadcrumb coating, deep-fried in oil, which closely matches the preparation of this dish. Minor adaptation applied: the dish is described as large shrimp (tiger/king prawn size) in a crispy batter, consistent with the USDA breaded fried shrimp profile. Per-100g values are taken directly from the USDA entry.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for battered deep-fried shrimp. GI estimated at 55 based on the analogous food category of battered/breaded fried seafood and wheat-flour-coated fried foods. Wheat flour batter is the primary glycemic contributor; GI of wheat-flour-based coatings is approximately 50–60 (low to medium range) per University of Sydney GI Database entries for similar battered products. Glycemic load per 150 g serving calculated as: (55 × 15.2 × 1.50) / 100 = 12.6 g.

hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size of crispy battered shrimp due to its moderate sodium content of approximately 430 mg per 100 g; a 150 g restaurant serving contributes around 645 mg sodium, representing over 25% of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (GI 55) and carbohydrate contribution from wheat-flour batter coating
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should be mindful that the wheat-flour batter coating contributes approximately 15 g of carbohydrate per 100 g (glycemic load ~12.6 per 150 g serving), placing this dish in the low glycemic index category; portion control and pairing with low-GI vegetables is recommended.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat (12.1 g/100 g), saturated fat (2.1 g/100 g), and cholesterol (152 mg/100 g) from deep-frying and shrimp
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume crispy battered shrimp in moderation due to its elevated cholesterol content (152 mg per 100 g) from shrimp and the added fat from deep-frying; choosing smaller portions and limiting frequency of consumption is advisable to manage saturated fat and dietary cholesterol intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Crispy Battered Shrimp (宫廷酥皮大明虾) is a Chinese-style deep-fried seafood dish featuring large tiger or king prawns encased in a light, crispy wheat-flour or breadcrumb batter and cooked in hot oil until golden and crunchy. The dish is popular in Chinese restaurants across Singapore and is associated with a regal or imperial culinary style, as suggested by the name '宫廷' (gōngtíng), meaning 'imperial court.' The texture is a satisfying contrast of a crunchy exterior and tender, juicy shrimp interior. The flavour profile is savoury with subtle sweetness from the shrimp, enhanced by seasoning in the batter. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 242 kcal, 17.5 g protein, 12.1 g fat (of which 2.1 g is saturated), and 15.2 g carbohydrate, with 430 mg sodium. The protein content is notable for a seafood dish, and shrimp is a good source of vitamin B12, selenium, phosphorus, and iodine. The deep-frying process adds significant fat and calories compared to steamed or grilled shrimp preparations. Clinically, the dish carries moderate concern for individuals managing hypertension (due to sodium), Type 2 Diabetes (due to the low-GI batter coating), and cardiovascular disease (due to cholesterol and fat from frying). It is suitable for halal diets and is dairy-free, but contains crustacean and gluten allergens. In Singapore, this dish is commonly served as a starter or sharing plate in Chinese seafood restaurants and zi char establishments.
common_user_queries: How many calories in crispy shrimp?
What is 宫廷酥皮大明虾?

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN entry exists for this specific dish. Nutrient values are adapted from the USDA SR Legacy entry for breaded fried shrimp (NDB 15150), which closely matches the preparation method. GI is an analogous estimate based on wheat-flour-battered fried seafood from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than high because no Singapore-specific primary source entry was found for this exact dish.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entry for breaded fried shrimp. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol, vitamin A, B-vitamins, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, and selenium values are taken from the USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for fried breaded shrimp and are considered reliable estimates for this dish category. The dish name '宫廷酥皮大明虾' translates literally as 'Imperial Court Crispy-Skin Large Bright Shrimp,' reflecting a premium restaurant presentation style. The serving size of 150 g is an estimate for a restaurant portion of 4–5 large shrimp with coating. Vitamin C is reported as 0.0 g; this is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central as shrimp prepared by deep-frying with batter contains negligible vitamin C. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate; a direct GI measurement for this specific dish has not been published in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of the processing date.
